name: Rust Macros
summary: Rust has two macro systems: declarative (`macro_rules!`) for pattern-based token-tree rewriting, and procedural for arbitrary token-stream manipulation. Proc macros are compiled as separate crates loaded by the compiler — not sandboxed by default. David Tolnay's Watt sandboxes them in WebAssembly for deterministic isolation.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Rust has two macro systems with very different shapes. **Declarative macros** (`macro_rules!`) do pattern-based rewriting on token trees: patterns match syntactic fragments (`$x:expr`, `$t:ty`, `$i:ident`) and templates substitute them into output tokens. They are hygienic via token-level binding tracking — names introduced in the macro do not collide with names at the call site.

**Procedural macros** are arbitrary Rust code that manipulates token streams. Three flavours: function-like (`foo!(...)`), derive (`#[derive(Foo)]`), and attribute (`#[foo] fn bar() {}`). The proc macro is compiled as a separate crate and loaded by the compiler during macro expansion; the target crate's tokens become a `TokenStream` argument.

sections.title: Declarative vs procedural
sections.content:
Declarative macros are constrained but predictable: hygienic, no I/O, no arbitrary code at compile time, recognisable to IDEs. Most macros in production Rust use this form — `vec!`, `println!`, `assert!`, `format!`, `dbg!`, and the bulk of library macros are `macro_rules!`.

Procedural macros are unconstrained but heavyweight: full Rust code runs during compilation, with access to filesystem, environment, and any library the proc macro crate depends on. Examples: `#[derive(Serialize)]` from serde inspects the target type's structure and emits a hand-quality serialiser; `#[tokio::main]` rewrites the function body to spin up a runtime; `sqlx::query!` parses an SQL string at compile time and types the result against a live database.

The trade-off is reach versus auditability. Declarative macros stay within the language's syntactic universe; procedural macros can do anything Rust can do at compile time, including non-determinism and side effects.

sections.title: Watt — proc macros sandboxed in Wasm
sections.content:
The architectural choice worth noting: proc macros are compiled as separate host crates and loaded by the compiler during expansion; they are not sandboxed by default. Tooling may execute them through a separate proc-macro server, and **Watt** (David Tolnay) compiles proc macros to WebAssembly for deterministic, sandboxed execution — trading a bit more latency for reproducibility and isolation.

The Watt design point is becoming more important as supply-chain concerns rise: a malicious proc macro can read filesystem, exfiltrate data, or alter generated code arbitrarily during compilation. Wasm-sandboxed proc macros bound the trust surface to the macro's declared inputs and the host's explicit grants.

sources.label: Watt — Rust proc macros in Wasm
